one_line_takeaway: A practitioner walkthrough showing that ART's standard adversarial attacks (FGSM/PGD/BIM/CW-L2/DeepFool/SimBA/Pixel) trivially fool a self-driving object detector and a bone-fracture CNN with tiny perturbations, while cheap input-preprocessing defenses recover under half the images.
key_finding: All tested attacks reliably broke both models with small perturbations. On object detection, PGD and BIM reached 100% success (mean perturbation 0.0639 and 0.0578) and FGSM 99% (0.0916); BIM was the stealthiest (lowest perturbation). On the bone-fracture CNN, DeepFool and BIM hit ~99% success, FGSM 95%, CW-L2 83%, Pixel 61%, and SimBA 47%; the Pixel attack was by far the least perceptible (mean perturbation 0.000185). Simple preprocessing defenses gave only partial protection: aggregate recovery rates were Total Variation denoising 42.7%, Median filtering 35.7%, Spatial smoothing 33.8%, Bilateral filtering 28%. Defenses worked well against low-perturbation attacks (Pixel: 98% recovered by both median filtering and spatial smoothing; CW-L2: 71-83%) but poorly against strong gradient attacks (BIM/FGSM often recovered only ~1%).
core_contribution: An empirical, practitioner-oriented benchmark from Infosys' Responsible AI Office that applies a battery of PRE-EXISTING adversarial attacks (all sourced from the IBM Adversarial Robustness Toolbox, ART v1.18.1) to two concrete ML use cases, then evaluates simple preprocessing defenses against them. The paper contributes no new attack or defense algorithm; its own contribution is (1) a hands-on comparison of attack effectiveness (success rate and mean perturbation) on an object-detection model and an image-classification model, and (2) a comparative evaluation of seven input-preprocessing defenses measured by post-defense recovery rate, perturbation mean, and PSNR. Note: the FGSM/PGD/BIM/CW-L2/DeepFool/SimBA/Pixel methods and the Szegedy/Goodfellow/Carlini-Wagner/Madry/Wong references in the introduction are CITED prior work, not this paper's inventions; the paper merely runs them via ART.
threat_model: Evasion attacks at inference time: an adversary crafts small pixel-level perturbations to input images to force misclassification or non-detection. Both white-box gradient-based attacks (FGSM, PGD, BIM, CW-L2, DeepFool, Pixel) and a query-based black-box attack (SimBA) are simulated. Motivating scenarios are safety-critical: fooling an autonomous-driving object detector (e.g., misreading road scenes) and corrupting a medical bone-fracture classifier. The paper frames business consequences (financial loss, reputational/regulatory harm) but does not formalize attacker capabilities/knowledge budgets beyond ART's default-vs-manually-tuned parameter settings.
limitations: Despite the title 'A Comprehensive Review,' this is a small hands-on demonstration, not a survey or a methodological advance: every attack and defense is off-the-shelf from ART/OpenCV/SciPy. Only 100 images per use case, drawn from single Kaggle datasets, so success-rate/perturbation numbers have wide uncertainty and no confidence intervals or repeated trials. 'Attack success rate' is defined loosely (adversarial sample generated) and mixes with 'defense recovery rate' semantics; for object detection, degradation is described qualitatively (objects missed/mislabeled) rather than with a detection metric like mAP drop. Attack settings are just 'default vs one manual tweak,' not a systematic sweep, making cross-attack comparisons apples-to-oranges (e.g., different eps, max_iter). Defenses are limited to input preprocessing (no adversarial training, certified defenses, or detection actually evaluated — those are only named). The introduction's business-impact and case-study material (Tesla stickers, Waymo) is narrative context, not evidence. PSNR/perturbation-mean tables are reported but not tied to a decision threshold. No adaptive-attack evaluation, so defense numbers likely overstate real robustness.

datasets_used: Kaggle Self-Driving Cars dataset (22,200 samples total; 100 randomly selected)
Kaggle Bone Break Classification Image dataset (10 fracture types; 100 samples selected)
COCO 2017 validation (referenced only as the source of DETR-ResNet50's reported AP 42.0)
quantitative_results: Object detection FGSM: 99% attack success, mean perturbation 0.0916007
Object detection PGD: 100% attack success, mean perturbation 0.063883
Object detection BIM: 100% attack success, mean perturbation 0.05783737
Bone-break Carlini-Wagner L2: 83% success, mean perturbation 0.0040231724
Bone-break Pixel attack: 61% success, mean perturbation 0.00018541123 (lowest perturbation of all)
Bone-break SimBA (simple black-box): 47% success, mean perturbation 0.007455054
DETR-ResNet50 reported clean AP 42.0 on COCO 2017 val; bone-break CNN ~99% clean accuracy
Median filtering aggregate defense recovery: 35.7% (per-attack: Pixel 98%, CW-L2 71%, SimBA 34%, DeepFool 9%, BIM 1%, FGSM 1%)
Total Variation denoising aggregate defense recovery: 42.7% (Pixel 90%, CW-L2 83%, Simba 36%, FGSM 22%, BIM 17%, DeepFool 8%)
Spatial smoothing aggregate defense recovery: 33.8% (Pixel 98%, CW-L2 70%, SimBA 25%, DeepFool 8%, BIM 1%, FGSM 1%)
Bilateral filtering aggregate defense recovery: 28% (Pixel 52%, CW-L2 49%, FGSM 34%, BIM 19%, DeepFool 8%, SimBA 6%)
PSNR of defense-applied images ranged ~75-87 dB (e.g., Pixel+median 86.77 dB, SimBA+bilateral 75.35 dB)
baselines_compared: Attacks compared against each other by success rate and mean perturbation (FGSM, PGD, BIM for detection; SimBA, FGSM, BIM, Pixel, DeepFool, CW-L2 for classification)

Defenses compared against each other by post-defense recovery rate, perturbation mean, and PSNR (Median Filtering, Total Variation denoising, Bilateral Filtering, Spatial Smoothing, Feature Squeezing, Gaussian Blur, Gaussian Smoothing)
Within each attack: ART default parameters vs manually tuned parameters (no external prior-work baseline reproduced for comparison)
